title: Add ProtocolCheckpoint component — summary + prediction + misconception at end of every protocol
description: T11 (Self-Test) and T12 (Summary) both score 0.00 across ALL 13 topics. This single component fixes both. Render after the final step of each protocol: (a) 3-bullet memorable summary, (b) one prediction question ('what would happen if...'), (c) the most common misconception for this protocol. Estimated impact: raises module teaching score from 31% to ~42%. No engine changes needed — purely a new UI component.
acceptanceCriteria: ProtocolCheckpoint renders after the last step of every protocol
Contains 3-bullet summary specific to the protocol just viewed
Contains 1 interactive prediction question with answer reveal
Contains 1 common misconception with explanation of why it's wrong
Content written for each of the 9 main protocol topics

title: Rewrite TCP descriptions with hook, phone-call analogy, and 'why 3 not 2' intuition
description: TCP scores 11/36 (D+). T1=0, T2=1, T8=0, T12=0. Current description: '3-way handshake, data transfer, 4-way teardown' — dry catalog entry. Rewrite with: HOOK: 'Every HTTP request pays a hidden tax before the first byte flows. That tax is the TCP handshake.' INTUITION: Phone call analogy — 'Can you hear me? / Yes, can you hear ME? / Yes.' Explains why 3 steps not 2. STEP DESCRIPTIONS: Add WHY each flag exists (SYN=synchronize sequence numbers to prevent injection, ACK=prove receipt). PERFORMANCE: '1 RTT NY-London = 56ms before any data. This is why connection pooling exists.' SUMMARY: 3 bullets. Source: Ilya Grigorik HPBN (hpbn.co/building-blocks-of-tcp/).
acceptanceCriteria: Protocol description in sidebar opens with motivating hook
Bottom panel or properties panel shows phone-call analogy before step 1
Step descriptions explain WHY each flag/number exists, not just WHAT
Performance section cites specific latency (56ms NY-London, 275% improvement from reuse)
Ends with 3-bullet summary

title: Rewrite DNS descriptions with hierarchy analogy and TTL production war stories
description: DNS scores 8/36 (D). T1=0, T2=0, T7=0, T8=0, T9=1. Worst teaching quality in the module after Protocol Deep-Dive. Rewrite with: HOOK: 'Every URL triggers an invisible quest — 4 servers, trillions of queries/day, and a misconfigured TTL can vanish your service for an hour.' INTUITION: Chain of address books analogy (root → TLD → auth = country directory → state directory → exact address). WHY HIERARCHICAL: 'No single server needs to know everything.' REAL-WORLD: GeoDNS for Netflix, CoreDNS in Kubernetes, TTL migration gotchas. MISTAKES: (1) DNS is not a single lookup, (2) recursive vs iterative confusion, (3) TTL propagation delays. Sources: Cloudflare learning center, howdns.works comic, Julia Evans DNS zine.
acceptanceCriteria: DNS description opens with hook about invisible quest and TTL danger
Address book analogy explains each server type's role before formal flow
Step descriptions explain WHY each server only knows one piece
Production section covers GeoDNS, Kubernetes CoreDNS, TTL migration
Common mistakes section covers 3 misconceptions

title: Rewrite CORS descriptions with doorman analogy and '7 common mistakes' approach
description: CORS scores 12/36 (C-). T1=1, T2=1, T8=1. The most misunderstood web security mechanism. Rewrite with: HOOK: 'At some point every developer stares at a CORS error — works in Postman, fails in browser. The confusion is not your fault.' INTUITION: Apartment doorman analogy — CORS blocks READING not SENDING (the truck still arrives). KEY INSIGHT: 'CORS does not prevent requests — it prevents JavaScript from reading the response.' MISTAKES: (1) thinking CORS blocks requests, (2) trying to fix it client-side, (3) mode:'no-cors' as a fix, (4) wildcard with credentials, (5) not handling OPTIONS. Sources: corsfix.com '7 Common Mistakes', Kevin Cox 'CORS is Stupid', Lydia Hallie CS Visualized.
acceptanceCriteria: Description opens with the relatable CORS error frustration hook
Doorman analogy explains CORS blocks reading not sending
Properties panel or bottom panel lists 5 common mistakes with explanations
Each CORS step explains the browser's internal decision logic
Performance section explains preflight overhead and Max-Age caching

title: Rewrite HTTP comparison with problem→solution narrative and real-world Cloudflare numbers
description: HTTP scores 12/36 (C-). T1=1, T2=1, T6=0, T8=0. Rewrite with: HOOK: 'In 1997, loading 100 images meant 100 TCP connections. The history of HTTP is a 25-year battle against this overhead.' INTUITION: Road→highway→tunnel analogy (HTTP/1.1=6 lanes, HTTP/2=multi-lane highway with all-lane-stop accident, HTTP/3=independent tunnels). NARRATIVE: Each version exists because the previous solution created the next problem. REAL NUMBERS: Cloudflare 176ms vs 201ms TTFB (12.4% improvement). KEY FACT: Server Push removed from Chrome v106 and Firefox v132. Sources: Accreditly comparison, Cloudflare blog, Smashing Magazine.
acceptanceCriteria: Description uses problem→solution chronological narrative
Road/highway/tunnel analogy appears before technical details
Production performance numbers cited (Cloudflare TTFB, Google search latency)
Server Push removal mentioned as interview-ready fact
Under packet loss scenario explains why HTTP/2 can be SLOWER than HTTP/1.1

title: Rewrite TLS descriptions with spy/paint-mixing analogy and forward secrecy explanation
description: TLS 1.3 scores 14/36 (C) — best step descriptions but weak hook and intuition. TLS 1.2 scores 11/36 (D+). Rewrite with: HOOK: 'Every padlock icon = a cryptographic negotiation in <100ms that no eavesdropper can break.' INTUITION: Paint-mixing analogy for Diffie-Hellman (each picks secret color, mixes publicly, arrives at same final color — observer can't reverse). FORWARD SECRECY: Explain WHY TLS 1.3 mandates ephemeral DH (RSA vulnerability scenario). MYTH-BUST: '<1% CPU overhead at Google scale.' REAL NUMBERS: 168ms→112ms→56ms progression (TLS 1.2→1.3→0-RTT). Sources: tls13.xargs.org, Ilya Grigorik HPBN, Auth0 TLS guide.
acceptanceCriteria: TLS description opens with padlock hook and real-world stakes
Paint-mixing or combination-lock analogy explains DH key exchange
Forward secrecy explanation shows WHY ephemeral keys prevent past decryption
Latency progression shown with named city pairs (56ms NY-London)
'TLS is not slow' myth-busting with Google's <1% CPU stat

title: Rewrite WebSocket descriptions with HTTP polling comparison and real-time framing
description: WebSocket scores 9/36 (D). T1=0, T2=0, T7=0. Description is purely mechanical. Rewrite with: HOOK: 'HTTP is like sending letters — you ask, you wait, you get a reply. WebSocket is a phone call — once connected, both sides talk freely.' COMPARISON: Show the overhead difference — 500-2000 bytes/request for HTTP polling vs 2-14 bytes/frame for WebSocket. WHY FULL-DUPLEX: Show chat app scenario where server needs to push immediately. REAL-WORLD: 'Slack uses WebSocket for 10M+ concurrent connections. Discord processes 1M events/sec over WebSocket.'
acceptanceCriteria: Description opens with letter vs phone-call analogy
Explicit overhead comparison: HTTP polling bytes vs WebSocket frame bytes
Step descriptions explain WHY upgrade mechanism exists (firewall transparency)
Real-world examples with specific companies and scale numbers
Comparison to SSE and long polling mentioned

title: Rewrite Serialization descriptions with 'every byte costs money at scale' hook and encoding intuition
description: Serialization scores 9/36 (D). T1=0, T2=0, T8=0. Current content shows size ratios but never explains WHY protobuf is smaller. Rewrite with: HOOK: 'At 1M events/sec to Kafka, the difference between JSON and Protobuf is $10K/month in bandwidth.' INTUITION: 'JSON sends the word name for every field: {name: Alice}. Protobuf sends a number: {1: Alice}. The field number is defined once in the schema, not repeated in every message.' WHY SCHEMA: 'Schema = compression dictionary shared between sender and receiver.' REAL-WORLD: Kafka+Avro, gRPC+Protobuf, Redis+MessagePack.
acceptanceCriteria: Description opens with cost-at-scale hook
Intuitive explanation of WHY binary is smaller (field tags vs field names)
Schema explained as 'shared compression dictionary' analogy
Real-world pairings cited (Kafka+Avro, gRPC+Protobuf)
Hex dump section labeled as simulated with explanation of what real encoding looks like

title: Rewrite CDN descriptions with geographic framing and cache math intuition
description: CDN scores 14/36 (C) — good scenario coverage but T1=0, T2=0, T8=0. Rewrite with: HOOK: 'Without a CDN, every user request travels to your origin server. Users in Tokyo wait 200ms for a server in Virginia. With a CDN, they get a response in 20ms from an edge in Tokyo.' INTUITION: Library branch analogy (origin=central library, edge=branch library, shield=regional warehouse). CACHE MATH: 'max-age=3600 with Age=3650 and stale-while-revalidate=60: 3650-3600=50 < 60, so serve stale. This ONE calculation determines whether your user waits 20ms or 200ms.' REAL-WORLD: Cloudflare, Fastly purge times.
acceptanceCriteria: Description opens with Tokyo vs Virginia latency comparison
Library branch analogy maps origin/shield/edge to real concept
SWR math explicitly walked through with the student
Real CDN providers named with specific purge propagation times
Cache-Control header syntax explained as a mini-language

title: Add common student mistakes section to all 9 protocol topics
description: T8 (Common Mistakes) averages 0.31/3 — nearly zero across the module. Research identified 3-5 specific mistakes per protocol from Reddit, Stack Overflow, and dev.to. Add a 'Common Mistakes' section to the properties panel for each protocol. TCP: confusing handshake ACK (increment by 1) with data ACK (increment by bytes). DNS: thinking it's a single lookup. CORS: thinking it blocks requests (it blocks response reading). HTTP: thinking HTTP/2 fully solves HOL blocking. TLS: thinking certificate encrypts data (it authenticates). WebSocket: thinking it's always better than HTTP. CDN: thinking cache miss is an error.
acceptanceCriteria: Each of 9 protocols has 2-3 common mistakes in properties panel
Each mistake explains what students think AND why it's wrong
Mistakes sourced from real student confusion (Reddit, forums)
Visual indicator draws attention to the mistakes section
Mistakes are protocol-specific, not generic

title: Rewrite Protocol Deep-Dive from RFC reference to teaching content
description: Protocol Deep-Dive scores 7/36 (F) — the only F in the module. Currently a wall of header fields, handshake steps, and use cases with zero pedagogy. It reads like an RFC excerpt. Rewrite each of the 10 protocols with: (1) one-paragraph 'what this protocol does and why' intro, (2) header fields grouped by purpose (identification, control, payload) instead of flat list, (3) handshake steps with WHY explanations, (4) performance characteristics explained intuitively ('1 RTT = 56ms NY-London'). The data structure (ProtocolDefinition) is fine — the content values need rewriting.
acceptanceCriteria: Each of 10 protocols has a 2-3 sentence intro explaining purpose and context
Header fields grouped by function with group labels
Handshake steps include WHY reasoning, not just WHAT
Performance numbers include concrete examples (city-pair latency)
Comparison notes rewritten to highlight the key trade-off decision

title: Add real-world latency numbers with named city pairs to all performance descriptions
description: T5 (Complexity/Performance Intuition) averages 1.62/3. Current content shows '1 RTT' or 'latency in milliseconds' without making it concrete. Research found the best explanations use named geography: '56ms NY-London', '200ms SF-Mumbai'. Add to TCP (handshake cost), TLS (1-RTT vs 2-RTT progression), DNS (cold vs warm resolution), HTTP (HOL blocking impact), CDN (edge vs origin latency). Source: Ilya Grigorik HPBN uses exact same technique.
acceptanceCriteria: At least 3 protocols cite specific RTT with named city pairs
TCP: '56ms NY-London × 1.5 RTT = 84ms before first data byte'
TLS: '168ms (TLS 1.2) → 112ms (TLS 1.3) → 56ms (0-RTT) on NY-London'
CDN: '200ms to origin in Virginia vs 20ms to edge in Tokyo'
Performance improvements quantified as percentages or multiples

title: Add REST vs GraphQL vs gRPC hook and over-fetching intuition
description: API Comparison scores 15/36 (C) — highest in module but still no hook (T1=1) and weak intuition (T2=1). The quantitative comparison is excellent but never explains WHY over-fetching is a problem before showing the numbers. Rewrite with: HOOK: 'Your mobile app needs 3 fields from a User object with 20 fields. REST sends all 20. GraphQL sends exactly 3. gRPC sends 3 as binary. At scale, this difference is millions of dollars in bandwidth.' Add explicit over-fetching demo showing wasted bytes highlighted.
acceptanceCriteria: Description opens with over-fetching cost hook
Visual shows REST response with wasted fields highlighted in gray
GraphQL response shows only requested fields with no waste
gRPC comparison shows binary size advantage
N+1 problem explained for REST before showing GraphQL solution

title: Add Connection Pool hook and 'why pooling exists' intuition
description: Connection Pool scores 11/36 (D+). T1=1, T2=1, T4=0 (zero step descriptions!). The simulation runs but never explains WHY connection pooling matters. Rewrite with: HOOK: 'Creating a new database connection costs 5-50ms. At 1000 requests/sec, that is 5-50 SECONDS of pure connection overhead per second — more time connecting than querying.' INTUITION: Taxi stand analogy (pool = fleet of waiting taxis vs hailing a new one each time). Add step-by-step event log explaining each pool action.
acceptanceCriteria: Description explains why connection creation is expensive
Taxi stand analogy shown before simulation starts
Event log panel shows each pool action with explanation
Metrics panel explains what 'pool exhaustion' means and why it matters
Comparison: 'with pooling' vs 'without pooling' performance numbers

title: Add Packet Journey hook and encapsulation-as-envelope analogy
description: Packet Journey scores 14/36 (C). T1=1, T4=1 — best visual metaphor in the module (T3=3) but weak descriptions and no summary. Rewrite with: HOOK: 'A single click on google.com triggers 12+ network operations across 7 layers. This simulator shows every one.' INTUITION: Russian nesting dolls / envelope analogy (HTTP request inside TCP segment inside IP packet inside Ethernet frame). Add layer-by-layer description as packet descends and ascends the stack.
acceptanceCriteria: Description opens with '12+ operations from 1 click' hook
Envelope/nesting-doll analogy explains encapsulation visually
Each hop has a 1-sentence WHY explanation in the timeline
TLS overhead clearly shown as 'additional cost for security'
Summary shows total latency breakdown by layer
